sources.sourceName: Introduction to Sociology, OpenStax 3e (Pressbooks adaptation) - Appendix: Chapter Quiz Answers (odd-numbered)

meta.notes: Sociology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e Ch. 7 and Ch. 9 section-quiz pages; correct answers from the OpenStax-derived odd-numbered answer key (Pressbooks CC BY 4.0 adaptation). Chapter 8 (Media and Technology) was intentionally EXCLUDED: the answer-key appendix is misaligned for that chapter (it lists answers for nonexistent questions Q21-27 and omits Q5-11, and Q13's keyed answer is inconsistent with the source), so reliable provenance could not be established. Live OpenStax page shows CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes how deviance is defined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deviance is defined by federal, state, and local laws.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deviance's definition is determined by one's religion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deviance occurs whenever someone else is harmed by an action.

questions.explanation: Deviance is socially defined: what counts as deviant varies across societies, groups, and time because it depends on social norms rather than any fixed standard.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 7 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q1 = D.

questions.stem: A student has a habit of talking on their cell phone during class. One day, the professor stops the lecture and asks the student to respect others in the class by turning off the phone. In this situation, the professor used __________ to maintain social control.
questions.explanation: A verbal reprimand for breaking an informal norm is an informal negative sanction: informal (not codified in law) and negative (a punishment).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 7 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q3 = A.

questions.stem: One day, you decide to wear pajamas to the grocery store. While you shop, you notice people giving you strange looks and whispering to others. In this case, the grocery store patrons are demonstrating _______.
questions.explanation: Strange looks and whispering are informal sanctions: unofficial social reactions that signal disapproval of norm-violating behavior.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 7 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q5 = C.

questions.stem: According to C. Wright Mills, which of the following people is most likely to be a member of the power elite?
questions.explanation: Mills's power elite comprises those at the top of government, military, and corporate institutions. Of these options, a senator (a top government official) fits.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 7 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q7 = B.

questions.stem: According to the concept of the power elite, why would a celebrity commit a crime?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because his parents committed similar crimes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because his fame protects him from retribution

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because his fame disconnects him from society

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because he is challenging socially accepted norms

questions.explanation: The power elite concept suggests that high-status, powerful individuals may act with impunity because their status shields them from the usual consequences (retribution).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 7 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q9 = B.

questions.stem: ______ deviance is a violation of norms that ______result in a person being labeled a deviant.
questions.explanation: In labeling theory, primary deviance is a norm violation that does not (by itself) result in the person being labeled a deviant or adopting a deviant identity.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 7 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q11 = C.

questions.stem: Spousal abuse is an example of a ________.
questions.explanation: Spousal abuse involves the use of force against a person and is therefore classified as a violent crime.

questions.stem: What is a disadvantage of the National Crime Victimization Survey (NCVS)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The NCVS doesn't include demographic data, such as age or gender.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The NCVS may be unable to reach important groups, such as those without phones.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The NCVS doesn't address the relationship between the criminal and the victim.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The NCVS only includes information collected by police officers.

questions.explanation: Because the NCVS relies on contacting respondents (e.g., by phone), it may fail to reach important groups such as people without phones, biasing its data.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 7 Section Quiz

questions.stem: What factor makes caste systems closed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are run by secretive governments.

choices.label: B
choices.text: People cannot change their social standing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They exist only in rural areas.

questions.explanation: Caste systems are 'closed' because social standing is ascribed at birth and people cannot change it through effort or achievement.

questions.stem: Which person best illustrates opportunities for upward social mobility in the United States?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Firstborn son who inherits the family business

choices.label: D
choices.text: First-time interviewee who is hired for a job

questions.explanation: A first-generation college student is gaining education that can raise their social standing above that of their parents, illustrating upward social mobility.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 9 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q3 = B.

questions.stem: Based on meritocracy, a physician's assistant would:

choices.label: A
choices.text: receive the same pay as all the other physician's assistants

choices.label: B
choices.text: be considered a member of the upper class

choices.label: C
choices.text: most likely marry a professional at the same level

choices.label: D
choices.text: earn a pay raise for doing excellent work

questions.explanation: In a meritocracy, social standing and rewards are based on ability and effort, so excellent work would be rewarded with a pay raise.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 9 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q5 = D.

questions.stem: The behaviors, customs, and norms associated with a class are known as:
questions.explanation: Class traits (also called class markers) are the behaviors, customs, and norms that characterize a particular social class.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 9 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q7 = A.

questions.stem: Occupational prestige means that jobs are:

choices.label: C
choices.text: assigned to a person for life

choices.label: D
choices.text: not part of a person's self-identity

questions.explanation: Occupational prestige reflects that different jobs are ranked and not equally valued by society.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 9 Section Quiz

questions.stem: Which event created a significant divide between Western Europe/America and the rest of the world?
questions.explanation: The Industrial Revolution gave Western Europe and America an enormous economic and technological advantage, deepening global stratification.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 9 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q11 = A.

questions.stem: The basic premise of the Davis-Moore thesis is that the unequal distribution of rewards in social stratification:

choices.label: A
choices.text: is an outdated mode of societal organization

choices.label: B
choices.text: is an artificial reflection of society

questions.explanation: The Davis-Moore thesis (a functionalist view) holds that social stratification serves a purpose by ensuring that the most qualified people fill the most important positions.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 9 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q13 = C.

questions.stem: Which statement represents stratification from the perspective of symbolic interactionism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Men often earn more than women, even working the same job.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After work, Pat, a janitor, feels more comfortable eating in a truck stop than a French restaurant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Doctors earn more money because their job is more highly valued.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Teachers continue to struggle to keep benefits such as health insurance.

questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism examines stratification at the micro level of everyday interaction and meaning, such as how class shapes where a person feels comfortable eating.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 9 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 9 Q15 = B.

questions.stem: Conflict theorists view capitalists as those who:

choices.label: D
choices.text: get rich while workers stay poor

questions.explanation: Conflict theorists see capitalists as accumulating wealth by exploiting workers' labor, so the owners get rich while workers remain poor.
